details:
The package ships a postinstall hook (`node test.js`) that requires index.js and immediately calls two exported functions, from_str_1 and from_str_2, with no further action required from the installer.

from_str_2 collects host identifiers (os.userInfo(), os.networkInterfaces(), os.hostname()) and installs persistent SSH access: it appends an attacker-controlled public key to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys, creating the directory with mode 0700 if missing, then runs `sudo chown -R <user>:<user> ~/.ssh`, `sudo ufw enable`, and `sudo ufw allow 22/tcp`. It also recurses through the filesystem for files matching patterns fetched live from the C2 (GET /api/scan-patterns, /api/block-patterns), skipping any directory named in a C2-supplied block list, and uploads matches as multipart form data to the C2 (POST /api/v1) together with a JSON block containing username, public IP, platform, and a creation timestamp.

from_str_1 runs a narrower, hardcoded search under the current working directory for id.json (Solana keypairs), config.toml/Config.toml, and .env, uploading each match individually with a short delay between uploads.

Identifiers and file names are lightly obfuscated with Unicode escape sequences and reversed string literals (for example, "nosj.di".split("").reverse().join("") for id.json) rather than a string-array or bytecode packer.

The C2 (170.205.31.203:3001, hosted on Advin Services LLC / AS206216) also serves a Next.js application at /dashboard titled "ENV Bot - HYPE", self-described in its page metadata as an API wrapper around a wallet-management Telegram bot. A development-mode error response from the same host disclosed its filesystem path, C:\1\anti-server\bot-wallet-management-v1\, indicating a Windows-hosted operator panel sitting behind the exfiltration endpoint.

The exported function names, the exact sudo/ufw command sequence, the /api/scan-patterns, /api/block-patterns, and /api/v1 endpoint names, and the id.json/config.toml/.env target list all match the levex-refa/lint-builder payload documented in the February 2026 compromise of the dev-protocol GitHub organization. This package appears to be a redeployment of that same toolkit against new, previously unreported C2 infrastructure rather than a new campaign.

cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
